blocks.text: When the season came, he sent a slave to the tenants to collect from them his share of the produce of the vineyard.
2 : , see 11.13n. In Mark, the “season” of God's kingdom is near (1.15). , or servant, a term often used for biblical prophets (e.g., Am 3.7; Zech 1.6).
But they seized him, and beat him, and sent him away empty-handed.
And again he sent another slave to them; this one they beat over the head and insulted.
Then he sent another, and that one they killed. And so it was with many others; some they beat, and others they killed.
3–5 : Through literary repetition, the parable emphasizes the motif of prophetic rejection (see 2 Chr 36.15–16).
He had still one other, a beloved son. Finally he sent him to them, saying, ‘They will respect my son.’
6 : , see 1.11; 9.7, where the term refers explicitly to Jesus (cf. 15.39).
But those tenants said to one another, ‘This is the heir; come, let us kill him, and the inheritance will be ours.’
7 : , recognition of the son's legitimate claim to the property and the tenants’ wanton designs on it; cf. Mt 25.34.
So they seized him, killed him, and threw him out of the vineyard.
What then will the owner of the vineyard do? He will come and destroy the tenants and give the vineyard to others.
9 : , language connoting triumph over evil (see 1.24; cf. 3.6). , probably righteous Gentiles, who assume their place in God's coming kingdom (see Mt 25.31–46).
Have you not read this scripture: ‘The stone that the builders rejected has become the cornerstone;

3.19b–35: Jesus at home (Mt 12.22–32; Lk 11.14–23; 12.10). An intertwined intercalation of two passages, linked by the motif of household. For other examples of this technique, see 5.21–43; 11.12–25[26]; 14.1–11.
this was the Lord's doing, and it is amazing in our eyes’?”
10–11 : An explanation of Jesus's rejection and vindication, perhaps also hinting at the inclusion of the “others” mentioned in 12.9. See Ps 118.22–23, also quoted in Acts 4.11; Eph 2.20; 1 Pet 2.7.
When they realized that he had told this parable against them, they wanted to arrest him, but they feared the crowd. So they left him and went away.
12.1–12: Parable of the wicked tenants (Mt 21.33–46; Lk 20.9–19).
Then they sent to him some Pharisees and some Herodians to trap him in what he said.

And they came and said to him, “Teacher, we know that you are sincere, and show deference to no one; for you do not regard people with partiality, but teach the way of God in accordance with truth. Is it lawful to pay taxes to the emperor, or not?
Should we pay them, or should we not?” But knowing their hypocrisy, he said to them, “Why are you putting me to the test? Bring me a denarius and let me see it.”
And they brought one. Then he said to them, “Whose head is this, and whose title?” They answered, “The emperor's.”
16 : , lit., “image.” Roman coins featured the head of the emperor. , lit., “inscription,” which read, “Tiberius Caesar, Son of the Divine Augustus, Augustus.”
Jesus said to them, “Give to the emperor the things that are the emperor's, and to God the things that are God's.” And they were utterly amazed at him.
17 : Jesus's ambiguous response leaves his opponents—and readers—to ponder the relationship between political and religious power. See Mt 17.24–27; Rom 13.1–7; 1 Pet 2.13–17. , see 1.22; 5.20.
Some Sadducees, who say there is no resurrection, came to him and asked him a question, saying,

18 : , a party of the wealthy elite. Since Annas II, high priest in 62 , was a Sadducee (Josephus, 20.197–99), his brother-in-law, Caiaphas, the high priest under whom Jesus is executed, probably was as well (see Acts 4.1; 5.17). Josephus says this party denied both the “traditions of the elders” (see 7.3n.) and the end-time resurrection of the dead (Josephus, 18.16).
“Teacher, Moses wrote for us that if a man's brother dies, leaving a wife but no child, the man shall marry the widow and raise up children for his brother.
19 : Deut 25.5–6 stipulates the practice of “levirate marriage” (see also Gen 38.8).
There were seven brothers; the first married and, when he died, left no children;
and the second married the widow and died, leaving no children; and the third likewise;
none of the seven left children. Last of all the woman herself died.
In the resurrection whose wife will she be? For the seven had married her.”
Jesus said to them, “Is not this the reason you are wrong, that you know neither the scriptures nor the power of God?
24 : , Jesus challenges both the Sadducees’ interpretive approach and their denial of God's coming reign; cf. 12.18n.
For when they rise from the dead, they neither marry nor are given in marriage, but are like angels in heaven.
25 : , those raised for the final judgment would inhabit spiritual bodies; see 1 Cor 15.35–50.
And as for the dead being raised, have you not read in the book of Moses, in the story about the bush, how God said to him, ‘I am the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob’?
He is God not of the dead, but of the living; you are quite wrong.”
12.18–27: A question about resurrection (Mt 22.23–33; Lk 20.27–40).
27 : , Jesus deflects attention from the Sadducees’ feigned interest in the afterlife to highlight God's activity in the present (see also 12.26; Ex 3.6,15–16).
One of the scribes came near and heard them disputing with one another, and seeing that he answered them well, he asked him, “Which commandment is the first of all?”
Jesus answered, “The first is, ‘Hear, O Israel: the Lord our God, the Lord is one;
you shall love the Lord your God with all your heart, and with all your soul, and with all your mind, and with all your strength.’
29–30 : Jesus cites the opening of the Shema, Deut 6.4–5, and adds “with all your mind.”
The second is this, ‘You shall love your neighbor as yourself.’ There is no other commandment greater than these.”
31 : Love of God and love of neighbor (Lev 19.18) are often linked; see also Rom 13.9; Gal 5.14; Jas 2.8).
Then the scribe said to him, “You are right, Teacher; you have truly said that ‘he is one, and besides him there is no other’;

and ‘to love him with all the heart, and with all the understanding, and with all the strength,’ and ‘to love one's neighbor as oneself,’—this is much more important than all whole burnt offerings and sacrifices.”
33 : Jesus echoes a common biblical view (see Hos 6.6).
When Jesus saw that he answered wisely, he said to him, “You are not far from the kingdom of God.” After that no one dared to ask him any question.
12.13–34: Jerusalem leaders attempt to ensnare Jesus. 12.13–17: A question about taxes (Mt 22.15–22; Lk 20.20–26).
34 : , possibly in two respects: Jesus thinks God's kingdom is drawing near (1.15; 9.1), and the scribe's answer reveals that he belongs to it.
While Jesus was teaching in the temple, he said, “How can the scribes say that the Messiah is the son of David?
35 : Jesus turns the questioning on his interrogators. , see 10.47n. Elsewhere, Christian writers emphasize Jesus's Davidic ties (see Mt 1.20; Lk 2.4; Rom 1.3).
David himself, by the Holy Spirit, declared, ‘The Lord said to my Lord, “Sit at my right hand, until I put your enemies under your feet.”’
David himself calls him Lord; so how can he be his son?” And the large crowd was listening to him with delight.

36–37 : Jesus cites Ps 110.1 as a proof text to deny the Messiah's Davidic lineage; elsewhere that passage is used of Jesus assuming a heavenly throne after the resurrection (see Acts 2.34–35; 1 Cor 15.25; Heb 1.13).
37 : , see 11.18n. This crowd's response may also reflect misplaced political hopes.
As he taught, he said, “Beware of the scribes, who like to walk around in long robes, and to be greeted with respect in the marketplaces,
38 : , distinctive attire of religious leaders. Elsewhere this critique is directed at the Pharisees (cf. Mt 23.1–36; Lk 11.37–44).
and to have the best seats in the synagogues and places of honor at banquets!
39 : , ancient meal practices designated one's status according to seating assignments; see Lk 14.7–11.
They devour widows’ houses and for the sake of appearance say long prayers. They will receive the greater condemnation.”
12.35–40: Jesus challenges the scribes (Mt 22.41–46; Lk 20.41–47).
40 : The prophets condemn those who take advantage of widows as weaker members of the social order (e.g., Isa 10.1–2; Zech 7.10; Mal 3.5; cf. Ps 94.1–7). , see Mt 6.5–6; 23.5–6.
He sat down opposite the treasury, and watched the crowd putting money into the treasury. Many rich people put in large sums.
A poor widow came and put in two small copper coins, which are worth a penny.
42 : The story highlights the exemplary devotion of a widow, whose social position is marginal (see 12.40n.). , the Greek calls the coinage a Roman , a tiny fraction of a laborer's daily earnings.
Then he called his disciples and said to them, “Truly I tell you, this poor widow has put in more than all those who are contributing to the treasury.
For all of them have contributed out of their abundance; but she out of her poverty has put in everything she had, all she had to live on.”
8.27–15.47: Jesus's messianic mission: sacrifice.
11.1–15.47: Jesus's destiny in Jerusalem.
11.1–13.37: Jesus in Jerusalem, during Passover.
12.41–44: A widow's lavish gift (Lk 21.1–4).
44 : Jesus contrasts giving out of with giving out of , lit., “her whole life”; see 8.35–36; cf. 10.22.
